title: Green oak post mallet
subtitle: A heavy driving mallet from a single green oak log section
excerpt: A post mallet for driving fence stakes: head and handle carved from a single green oak log section, 60 cm long and 15 cm in diameter. Rived to shape with a froe and side axe, then drawknifed to the final profile. Green oak is the traditional British post-mallet wood; dense enough to drive a 5 cm stake in six blows.
sourceNotes: Traditional British farm and woodland tool construction; Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for single-piece mallet proportions.

glossaryTerms.definition: Freshly felled or recently cut timber, still high in moisture content. Green oak is much easier to work with hand tools than seasoned oak.
An L-shaped cleaving tool: a blade driven into the end grain with a mallet, then levered to split wood along the grain.
Splitting wood along the grain using a froe and mallet. Rived timber is stronger than sawn because the grain is unbroken across the full length.

content.text: A post mallet from a single log section is a straightforward piece of green woodwork: the head mass comes from the log diameter, the handle is riven from the same wood, and the one-piece construction means there is no joint to fail.
Green wood

oak is the best material; dense enough for driving work, and far more workable under hand tools than seasoned oak. The mallet will harden and check slightly as it dries, which only increases the density of the head.
The wood

A section of freshly felled oak: 60 cm long, 14 to 16 cm diameter. The section should have straight grain running along the length with no major knots in the handle zone (the lower 40 cm). Anything from a felling or a large branch from storm damage works; a post mallet does not require premium timber. Hornbeam and beech are alternatives: both are very hard and produce good driving mallets.
Tools
A
froe
and beetle mallet for the
riving

split. A side axe to remove the bulk of the handle waste. A drawknife on a shaving horse to refine the handle profile. A sloyd knife for chamfering the grip end. The side axe must be sharp; it does the heaviest single step in the project.
Method

Stand the log section on end on a chopping block. Mark the head zone: the top 20 cm, left as the full log diameter. Mark the handle waste zone: lines on the end grain showing the 4 cm wide handle that will remain within the lower 40 cm of the log. The handle is centred in the section.

Rive away the handle waste with the froe. Set the froe blade on the end grain at the handle waste boundary line, 2 cm from the handle centre mark. Drive it with the beetle, then lever to split the waste section free. Repeat on the opposite side. The riving removes the bulk of the wood either side of the handle in two splits; the remaining waste is axe work.

Work the handle profile with the side axe: bring the rough sides down to within 5 mm of the intended handle width. Axe at a low angle, cutting with the grain. Do not try to bring the handle to finished dimensions with the axe; leave material for the drawknife.

Secure the mallet in the shaving horse and refine the handle with the drawknife. The finished handle is 4 cm wide, 3 cm thick, and slightly oval. Leave the neck; the junction between handle and head; full-width for 5 cm below the head face. This provides mass at the top of the swing arc.

Shape the head top with the side axe: a slight dome on the striking face (2 cm crown) prevents the mallet deflecting off-centre on an angled post top. The head sides are left round; the natural log curve; which is the traditional form for a post mallet.

Season the mallet for 6 weeks before hard use. The oak head will check slightly at the end grain as it dries; these surface checks do not affect performance. Do not attempt to dry the mallet quickly; rapid drying causes deeper checks that can split the head.
Finishing

Leave unfinished. A post mallet is a working outdoor tool; any oil finish on the striking face softens on contact with moisture and reduces grip on the post. The handle may be oiled with boiled linseed oil if comfort is a concern, but this is optional.
Variations
Hornbeam: the hardest common British woodland species. Produces a heavier head for the same diameter; excellent for driving hardwood posts.
Lighter version: a 10 cm diameter section, 50 cm long. More comfortable for one-hand use; suitable for tent pegs and small garden stakes rather than fence posts.
Care

Store dry between uses. The head will harden with age as the moisture content equilibrates. A mallet that has dried completely is harder and heavier-feeling than a green one; this is normal and desirable.

attrs.heading: What can go wrong
attrs.intro: Common problems with the green oak post mallet.

items.symptom: Froe split runs past the handle zone into the head
items.cause: Grain not perfectly straight, or too much lever pressure in one direction
items.fix: Work in short increments; 10 cm at a time; and check the split direction before proceeding. Lever against the side where the split is running correctly to steer it back.

items.symptom: Deep longitudinal checks appear in the head during drying
items.cause: Drying too quickly in direct sun or near a heat source
items.fix: Season in a cool, shaded place with some air movement. A check that goes more than 1 cm deep and runs into the neck area can be stabilised by wrapping the head with two steel ring clamps while it finishes drying.

items.symptom: Handle breaks at the neck on first hard use
items.cause: Neck too thin, or knot in the wood at the neck junction
items.fix: The neck should be no less than 4 cm at its narrowest point. Check for knots before starting; a knot at the neck is a structural failure point under driving loads.
